title: Type 2 Diabetes Mellitus
oneLiner: Chronic insulin resistance & relative beta-cell failure; common, progressive, and a major cause of CV disease, renal failure, and amputation.

sections.Killers First — What Will Kill Them In T:
- **Hypoglycaemia** (often iatrogenic): confused, sweaty, agitated; capillary glucose <3.0 mmol/L — treat immediately with 15g fast-acting CHO or 1mg IM glucagon before any other history or tests.
- **Hyperosmolar Hyperglycaemic State (HHS)** (more common in T2DM): drowsy, profoundly dehydrated, glucose often >40 mmol/L, no/ scant ketones; requires aggressive IV fluids (NOT just insulin — fluid first) and careful monitoring.
- **Diabetic Ketoacidosis (DKA)** (less common but possible, especially with infection / missed insulin): fruity breath, Kussmaul breathing, ketonaemia >3 mmol/L; capillary ketones >3 mmol/L + glucose >14 mmol/L → start fixed-rate insulin infusion per local DKA protocol.
sections.Presentation:
- Often asymptomatic → incidental finding on routine bloods
- Polyuria, polydipsia, nocturia, weight loss (less dramatic than T1DM)
- Recurrent infections (UTI, thrush, cellulitis)
- Blurred vision, fatigue, slow healing wounds
- Exam: raised BMI, acanthosis nigricans, peripheral neuropathy (absent ankle jerks, reduced vibration sense), foot ulcers, retinopathy on fundoscopy
sections.Differential Diagnosis:
- **Type 1 Diabetes** — younger, leaner, rapid onset, ketosis-prone; autoantibodies positive (GAD, IA2); absolute insulin deficiency
- **Latent Autoimmune Diabetes in Adults (LADA)** — older T1DM variant; slow onset, low C-peptide, autoantibodies positive
- **Maturity Onset Diabetes of the Young (MODY)** — strong family history (autosomal dominant), onset <25 years, low BMI; genetic testing for specific mutations
- **Secondary diabetes** — pancreatitis, pancreatic cancer, cystic fibrosis, Cushing's, acromegaly, steroid therapy
- **Gestational diabetes** — onset during pregnancy, resolves postpartum
- **Splitter:** Ketonaemia >3 mmol/L + low C-peptide (<200) → T1DM/LADA, not T2DM
sections.Investigations:
- **Bedside:** capillary glucose (fingerstick) → if >11.1 mmol/L random, do venous glucose; urine dip for glucose and ketones; capillary ketones if unwell
- **Bloods:** FBC, U&E, LFT, glucose, HbA1c, fasting lipids, TSH (if weight gain or lipid issues)
- **Imaging:** Not routine; if suspect pancreatitis → CT abdomen; echocardiogram if new heart failure / atherosclerosis
- **Special tests:** Anti-GAD antibodies (if T1DM/LADA suspected); C-peptide (fasting/ stimulated) to differentiate insulin deficiency vs resistance
- **Diagnostic criteria** (WHO/NICE):
  - Fasting plasma glucose ≥7.0 mmol/L (after 8h fast) OR
  - HbA1c ≥48 mmol/mol (6.5%) — confirmed on repeat (unless symptomatic + random glucose >11.1) OR
  - 2h glucose ≥11.1 mmol/L after 75g OGTT
- **Never-miss test:** Urine dip for ketones in any unwell diabetic; if positive → check capillary ketones — missing DKA/HHS is fatal
sections.Management:
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**
  - If hypoglycaemic: give 15g rapid-acting carbohydrate (glucose tablets, 150ml fruit juice, 4 sugar lumps) → recheck in 10–15 min. If not safe PO: 1mg IM glucagon or 50ml 50% dextrose IV (preferably via large vein).
  - If DKA/HHS suspected: start IV fluids (0.9% saline 500–1000ml stat then per local protocol), contact diabetes team, arrange HDU/ITU if severe.
  - If stable hyperglycaemia (glucose >15 mmol/L, no ketones, well): no urgent intervention; start oral agent plan.
- **Ward (first 24-72h):**
  - Review medication adherence, infection screen (chest X-ray, urine culture, blood cultures if febrile).
  - Start / titrate insulin if acutely unwell, peri-operative, or on steroids (commonly 30–50 units total daily dose of basal insulin e.g., insulatard or glargine; adjust per ward protocol).
  - IV insulin sliding scales if NBM / peri-operative — ensure co-prescribed dextrose to avoid hypoglycaemia.
- **Long-term / outpatient:**
  - Lifestyle: weight loss (5–10% body weight → often remission), diet (low sugar, high fibre), exercise (150 min/week).
  - First-line: Metformin (standard release 500mg BD, titrate to 1g BD) if eGFR >30.
  - Second-line: Add SGLT2 inhibitor (dapagliflozin 10mg OD) or GLP-1 receptor agonist (liraglutide 1.2–1.8mg SC OD) — favoured if high CV or renal risk.
  - Third-line: Add DPP-4 inhibitor (sitagliptin 100mg OD) or sulfonylurea (gliclazide MR 30–60mg OD) or insulin.
  - Annual review: foot check (monofilament + pulses), retinal photography, urine ACR, CKD screen, lipid profile.
- **Default Orders (if admitted):**
  - **Fluids:** Usually no fluid restriction unless HHS/DKA; otherwise keep well hydrated with oral fluids. If NBM: IV dextrose 5% + KCl (10 mmol per bag) if on insulin.
  - **VTE prophylaxis:** Enoxaparin 40mg SC OD (if weight >120kg consider 40mg BD or [check local policy]).
  - **Diet:** Diabetic diet or standard hospital diet; if on insulin, ensure meal timing matches insulin peaks.
  - **Obs:** Document capillary glucose before meals and at bedtime; more frequently if unstable.
sections.Drugs & Doses:
- **Metformin:** Start 500mg PO once daily with food; increase to 1g BD as tolerated (max 2.5g daily). Contraindicated if eGFR <30; avoid temporarily if IV contrast.
- **Gliclazide MR:** 30–60mg PO once daily with breakfast. Avoid if significant liver impairment; caution with elderly.
- **Dapagliflozin (SGLT2 inhibitor):** 10mg PO once daily. Hold if acute illness / vomiting / NBM (risk of euglycaemic DKA); avoid if eGFR <25.
- **Liraglutide (GLP-1 agonist):** Start 0.6mg SC once daily, titrate weekly to max 1.8mg OD. GI side effects; caution if hx pancreatitis.
- **Insulin glargine (long-acting):** Starting dose 10–20 units SC once daily, titrate by 2–4 units every 2–3 days if fasting glucose >10 mmol/L. Alternative: isophane (Humulin I) 20 units BD.
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:** Metformin — stop if eGFR <30, consider reduced dose if eGFR 30–45. Sulphonylureas — accumulate in CKD → increased hypoglycaemia risk. SGLT2i — avoid if eGFR <25. GLP-1 analogues — limited data in severe CKD; liraglutide avoid if eGFR <15.
- **Most dangerous dose error:** **Insulin:** Never give a bolus of rapid-acting insulin without checking current glucose and whether the patient is about to eat. A common fatal error is "giving the sliding scale insulin without checking the meal has arrived" or "giving IV insulin bolus in DKA before checking potassium" (can cause fatal arrhythmia). Second: **Metformin** continued in eGFR <30 → risk of lactic acidosis.
sections.Complications:
- **Acute:**
  - Hypoglycaemia (iatrogenic, especially with sulphonylureas / insulin)
  - Hyperglycaemic emergencies (DKA — less common in T2DM; HHS — more common)
  - Infection (foot ulcers, UTI, pneumonia, sepsis)
- **Chronic:**
  - **Microvascular:** Diabetic retinopathy, nephropathy (microalbuminuria → proteinuria → CKD), neuropathy (sensory > motor, autonomic → gastroparesis, postural hypotension)
  - **Macrovascular:** Accelerated atherosclerosis → MI, stroke, PAD
  - **Diabetic foot disease:** neuropathy + ischaemia → ulceration, infection, amputation
sections.Prognosis: - Chronic progressive condition; glycaemic control and risk factor management (BP, lipids, smoking) significantly reduce complications. Life expectancy reduced by ~6 years in T2DM at age 50; CVD is leading cause of death.
sections.Pearls (positive actions):
- **DO** check urine ACR (albumin:creatinine ratio) annually from diagnosis — early nephropathy detection allows ACEi/ARB benefit.
- **DO** a documented foot examination at every admission (inspect soles, between toes, monofilament test, pedal pulses) — prevent amputation.
- **DO** start metformin at low dose (500mg OD) and titrate slowly to minimise GI side effects; if poorly tolerated, switch to MR formulation.
sections.Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid):
- **NOT** discharging with unchanged regimen if admitted with acute hyperglycaemia and new infection — they often need temporary insulin or dose adjustment.
- **NOT** assuming all hyperglycaemia is T2DM — especially in younger (<40) or lean patients; check antibodies and C-peptide.
- **NOT** using sulphonylureas or insulin in patients at high risk of falls (elderly, frail) without robust hypoglycaemia monitoring.
sections.Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home):
- **Return if:** Glucose persistently >20 mmol/L despite increased medication, symptoms of DKA (nausea, vomiting, abdominal pain, rapid breathing, confusion) or hypoglycaemia <3.0 mmol/L not responding to oral treatment.
- **Follow-up:** GP review within 1 week for medication adjustment; diabetic foot clinic if any ulcer; retinal screening within 3 months if not up to date.
sections.Escalation Criteria:
- **Call senior / diabetes specialist nurse if:** Glucose >30 mmol/L + ketones >1.5 mmol/L, any alteration in consciousness, NEWS2 ≥5, lactate >4 mmol/L, eGFR drop >20% from baseline, or unable to achieve glycaemic control on ward with oral agents alone (consider insulin).
- **Call ICU / MET if:** HHS with GCS <12, DKA with acidosis (pH <7.2, HCO3 <10), severe hypoglycaemia (glucose <2.0) not resolving with glucagon/D50, or any diabetic emergency with haemodynamic instability (SBP <90 mmHg).

rawMarkdown:
## Type 2 Diabetes Mellitus
**One-liner:** Chronic insulin resistance & relative beta-cell failure; common, progressive, and a major cause of CV disease, renal failure, and amputation.

### Killers First — What Will Kill Them In The First Hour?
- **Hypoglycaemia** (often iatrogenic): confused, sweaty, agitated; capillary glucose <3.0 mmol/L — treat immediately with 15g fast-acting CHO or 1mg IM glucagon before any other history or tests.
- **Hyperosmolar Hyperglycaemic State (HHS)** (more common in T2DM): drowsy, profoundly dehydrated, glucose often >40 mmol/L, no/ scant ketones; requires aggressive IV fluids (NOT just insulin — fluid first) and careful monitoring.
- **Diabetic Ketoacidosis (DKA)** (less common but possible, especially with infection / missed insulin): fruity breath, Kussmaul breathing, ketonaemia >3 mmol/L; capillary ketones >3 mmol/L + glucose >14 mmol/L → start fixed-rate insulin infusion per local DKA protocol.

### Presentation
- Often asymptomatic → incidental finding on routine bloods
- Polyuria, polydipsia, nocturia, weight loss (less dramatic than T1DM)
- Recurrent infections (UTI, thrush, cellulitis)
- Blurred vision, fatigue, slow healing wounds
- Exam: raised BMI, acanthosis nigricans, peripheral neuropathy (absent ankle jerks, reduced vibration sense), foot ulcers, retinopathy on fundoscopy

### Differential Diagnosis
- **Type 1 Diabetes** — younger, leaner, rapid onset, ketosis-prone; autoantibodies positive (GAD, IA2); absolute insulin deficiency
- **Latent Autoimmune Diabetes in Adults (LADA)** — older T1DM variant; slow onset, low C-peptide, autoantibodies positive
- **Maturity Onset Diabetes of the Young (MODY)** — strong family history (autosomal dominant), onset <25 years, low BMI; genetic testing for specific mutations
- **Secondary diabetes** — pancreatitis, pancreatic cancer, cystic fibrosis, Cushing's, acromegaly, steroid therapy
- **Gestational diabetes** — onset during pregnancy, resolves postpartum
- **Splitter:** Ketonaemia >3 mmol/L + low C-peptide (<200) → T1DM/LADA, not T2DM

### Investigations
- **Bedside:** capillary glucose (fingerstick) → if >11.1 mmol/L random, do venous glucose; urine dip for glucose and ketones; capillary ketones if unwell
- **Bloods:** FBC, U&E, LFT, glucose, HbA1c, fasting lipids, TSH (if weight gain or lipid issues)
- **Imaging:** Not routine; if suspect pancreatitis → CT abdomen; echocardiogram if new heart failure / atherosclerosis
- **Special tests:** Anti-GAD antibodies (if T1DM/LADA suspected); C-peptide (fasting/ stimulated) to differentiate insulin deficiency vs resistance
- **Diagnostic criteria** (WHO/NICE):
  - Fasting plasma glucose ≥7.0 mmol/L (after 8h fast) OR
  - HbA1c ≥48 mmol/mol (6.5%) — confirmed on repeat (unless symptomatic + random glucose >11.1) OR
  - 2h glucose ≥11.1 mmol/L after 75g OGTT
- **Never-miss test:** Urine dip for ketones in any unwell diabetic; if positive → check capillary ketones — missing DKA/HHS is fatal

### Management
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**
  - If hypoglycaemic: give 15g rapid-acting carbohydrate (glucose tablets, 150ml fruit juice, 4 sugar lumps) → recheck in 10–15 min. If not safe PO: 1mg IM glucagon or 50ml 50% dextrose IV (preferably via large vein).
  - If DKA/HHS suspected: start IV fluids (0.9% saline 500–1000ml stat then per local protocol), contact diabetes team, arrange HDU/ITU if severe.
  - If stable hyperglycaemia (glucose >15 mmol/L, no ketones, well): no urgent intervention; start oral agent plan.
- **Ward (first 24-72h):**
  - Review medication adherence, infection screen (chest X-ray, urine culture, blood cultures if febrile).
  - Start / titrate insulin if acutely unwell, peri-operative, or on steroids (commonly 30–50 units total daily dose of basal insulin e.g., insulatard or glargine; adjust per ward protocol).
  - IV insulin sliding scales if NBM / peri-operative — ensure co-prescribed dextrose to avoid hypoglycaemia.
- **Long-term / outpatient:**
  - Lifestyle: weight loss (5–10% body weight → often remission), diet (low sugar, high fibre), exercise (150 min/week).
  - First-line: Metformin (standard release 500mg BD, titrate to 1g BD) if eGFR >30.
  - Second-line: Add SGLT2 inhibitor (dapagliflozin 10mg OD) or GLP-1 receptor agonist (liraglutide 1.2–1.8mg SC OD) — favoured if high CV or renal risk.
  - Third-line: Add DPP-4 inhibitor (sitagliptin 100mg OD) or sulfonylurea (gliclazide MR 30–60mg OD) or insulin.
  - Annual review: foot check (monofilament + pulses), retinal photography, urine ACR, CKD screen, lipid profile.
- **Default Orders (if admitted):**
  - **Fluids:** Usually no fluid restriction unless HHS/DKA; otherwise keep well hydrated with oral fluids. If NBM: IV dextrose 5% + KCl (10 mmol per bag) if on insulin.
  - **VTE prophylaxis:** Enoxaparin 40mg SC OD (if weight >120kg consider 40mg BD or [check local policy]).
  - **Diet:** Diabetic diet or standard hospital diet; if on insulin, ensure meal timing matches insulin peaks.
  - **Obs:** Document capillary glucose before meals and at bedtime; more frequently if unstable.

### Drugs & Doses
- **Metformin:** Start 500mg PO once daily with food; increase to 1g BD as tolerated (max 2.5g daily). Contraindicated if eGFR <30; avoid temporarily if IV contrast.
- **Gliclazide MR:** 30–60mg PO once daily with breakfast. Avoid if significant liver impairment; caution with elderly.
- **Dapagliflozin (SGLT2 inhibitor):** 10mg PO once daily. Hold if acute illness / vomiting / NBM (risk of euglycaemic DKA); avoid if eGFR <25.
- **Liraglutide (GLP-1 agonist):** Start 0.6mg SC once daily, titrate weekly to max 1.8mg OD. GI side effects; caution if hx pancreatitis.
- **Insulin glargine (long-acting):** Starting dose 10–20 units SC once daily, titrate by 2–4 units every 2–3 days if fasting glucose >10 mmol/L. Alternative: isophane (Humulin I) 20 units BD.
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:** Metformin — stop if eGFR <30, consider reduced dose if eGFR 30–45. Sulphonylureas — accumulate in CKD → increased hypoglycaemia risk. SGLT2i — avoid if eGFR <25. GLP-1 analogues — limited data in severe CKD; liraglutide avoid if eGFR <15.
- **Most dangerous dose error:** **Insulin:** Never give a bolus of rapid-acting insulin without checking current glucose and whether the patient is about to eat. A common fatal error is "giving the sliding scale insulin without checking the meal has arrived" or "giving IV insulin bolus in DKA before checking potassium" (can cause fatal arrhythmia). Second: **Metformin** continued in eGFR <30 → risk of lactic acidosis.

### Complications
- **Acute:**
  - Hypoglycaemia (iatrogenic, especially with sulphonylureas / insulin)
  - Hyperglycaemic emergencies (DKA — less common in T2DM; HHS — more common)
  - Infection (foot ulcers, UTI, pneumonia, sepsis)
- **Chronic:**
  - **Microvascular:** Diabetic retinopathy, nephropathy (microalbuminuria → proteinuria → CKD), neuropathy (sensory > motor, autonomic → gastroparesis, postural hypotension)
  - **Macrovascular:** Accelerated atherosclerosis → MI, stroke, PAD
  - **Diabetic foot disease:** neuropathy + ischaemia → ulceration, infection, amputation

### Prognosis
- Chronic progressive condition; glycaemic control and risk factor management (BP, lipids, smoking) significantly reduce complications. Life expectancy reduced by ~6 years in T2DM at age 50; CVD is leading cause of death.

### Pearls (positive actions)
- **DO** check urine ACR (albumin:creatinine ratio) annually from diagnosis — early nephropathy detection allows ACEi/ARB benefit.
- **DO** a documented foot examination at every admission (inspect soles, between toes, monofilament test, pedal pulses) — prevent amputation.
- **DO** start metformin at low dose (500mg OD) and titrate slowly to minimise GI side effects; if poorly tolerated, switch to MR formulation.

### Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid)
- **NOT** discharging with unchanged regimen if admitted with acute hyperglycaemia and new infection — they often need temporary insulin or dose adjustment.
- **NOT** assuming all hyperglycaemia is T2DM — especially in younger (<40) or lean patients; check antibodies and C-peptide.
- **NOT** using sulphonylureas or insulin in patients at high risk of falls (elderly, frail) without robust hypoglycaemia monitoring.

### Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home)
- **Return if:** Glucose persistently >20 mmol/L despite increased medication, symptoms of DKA (nausea, vomiting, abdominal pain, rapid breathing, confusion) or hypoglycaemia <3.0 mmol/L not responding to oral treatment.
- **Follow-up:** GP review within 1 week for medication adjustment; diabetic foot clinic if any ulcer; retinal screening within 3 months if not up to date.

### Escalation Criteria
- **Call senior / diabetes specialist nurse if:** Glucose >30 mmol/L + ketones >1.5 mmol/L, any alteration in consciousness, NEWS2 ≥5, lactate >4 mmol/L, eGFR drop >20% from baseline, or unable to achieve glycaemic control on ward with oral agents alone (consider insulin).
- **Call ICU / MET if:** HHS with GCS <12, DKA with acidosis (pH <7.2, HCO3 <10), severe hypoglycaemia (glucose <2.0) not resolving with glucagon/D50, or any diabetic emergency with haemodynamic instability (SBP <90 mmHg).
